subject: Gastroenterology
question: A 45-year-old woman with gallstones presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to back, nausea, and vomiting after eating fatty meal. Lipase 3500 U/L. What is the best initial management?

options.explanation: Acute pancreatitis management: aggressive IV fluids (LR preferred), NPO (bowel rest), IV opioids for pain. Most cases mild and self-limited. Identify and treat etiology (gallstones). ERCP if cholangitis present.
ERCP indicated for gallstone pancreatitis WITH cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction. Not routine for all pancreatitis. Timing: within 24-48h for urgent indications.
Prophylactic antibiotics NOT recommended in acute pancreatitis (even severe). Only if infected necrosis confirmed (CT-guided aspiration). Overuse promotes resistant organisms.
Cholecystectomy delayed until pancreatitis resolves (same admission for mild cases). Immediate surgery risks worsening inflammation and complications.
Octreotide not shown to improve outcomes in acute pancreatitis. No longer recommended. Supportive care (fluids, NPO, analgesia) remains standard.

educationalObjective: Manage acute pancreatitis supportively: aggressive IV fluids, NPO, pain control. ERCP only if cholangitis. Antibiotics only if infected necrosis.
keyTakeaway: Pancreatitis: Fluids, NPO, pain control. Lipase >3x normal = diagnostic. Ranson/Glasgow criteria for severity.
